column2: Proto-Elamites (4000–3000 BCE): Developed early writing system and trade networks
Elamite Wars (2700–1600 BCE): Fought Assyrians/Babylonians for control of Susiana plain
Indo-Iranian Migration: Brought horse domestication and chariot warfare to region
Median Innovations: Cyaxares' introduction of armored cavalry and siege engines
Persian Adaptations: Cyrus' combined arms (camels, sparabara shield-bearers, horse archers)
Logistical Mastery: Darius' 2,500 km Royal Road enabled rapid troop movements
Administration: Cyrus' Cyrus Cylinder (first human rights charter)
Religion: Zoroastrianism became state doctrine under Darius
Architecture: Persepolis' Apadana Palace showcased imperial unity

column3: Used cats as shields (Egyptian religious tactic)
column4: Added Nile Valley's resources to empire

column2: This table spans from prehistoric settlements to Darius I's administrative reforms, showing how successive Iranian peoples built military capabilities that culminated in history's first superpower. The adjusted years follow BCE dating (e.g., 550 BCE = -550).
